coredata.dc:title: Cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets integrating with metal indicator enable sensitive detection of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinli
Jiao, Lei
Xu, Weiqing
Yan, Hongye
Chen, Guojuan
Wu, Yu
Hu, Liuyong
Gu, Wenling
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) plays a significant role in various biological functions. The fluctuation of GSH level is closely linked to some serious diseases. Currently, the colorimetric detection of GSH is mainly built on the inhibitory effect on the catalytic activity of the developed nanomaterials. However, tedious synthesis of the nanomaterials is always required to control their morphologies for better catalytic activity. Metal indicators are some specific substances capable of chelating metal ions, during which significant color variation can be triggered, making them promising for colorimetric analysis. More importantly, the recognition of metal ions has nothing to do with the structure of the materials. Herein, CoOOH ultrathin nanosheets were prepared via a facile method, which can be easily reduced to Co2+ ions by GSH. After being chelated by metal indicator, a rapid and sensitive sensing platform for GSH detection is established with the distinct color change. As a result, the UV–vis absorbances of Co2+–xylenol orange disodium salt complexes (a typical metal indicator) at 580 nm exhibit good linearity against GSH concentrations from 33 μM to 1.3 mM with the detection limit of 13.7 μM. The developed sensor is further used in the detection of GSH contained in the human serum and an ideal recovery rate is obtained, demonstrating its excellent applicability for the detection of GSH in practical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric detection
Biosensors
Cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets
Metal indicator
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric sensing of GSH in vitro
Detection of GSH in serum samples
Color changes of metal indicator in the presence of metal ions
Detection of GSH in serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (A inset) Digital picture of the Tyndall effect, (B) HRTEM image and (C) SAED pattern of the obtained CoOOH. (D) HAADF-STEM image and HAADF-STEM mapping images of (E) Co and (F) O.
(A) XRD pattern of the as-prepared CoOOH, (B) FT-IR and (B inset) UV–vis absorbance, high-resolution XPS spectra of (C) Co 2p and (D) O 1s of CoOOH.

(A) Absorbance variation of PAN, EBT and XO after been complexed with Co2+. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of XO complexed with different concentrations of Co2+. (C) Time-absorbance curves of TMB oxidized by H2O2 catalyzed by HRP and XO complexed with Co2+.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH, CoOOH + XO, CoOOH + GSH + XO. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH + XO in the presence of different concentrations of GSH and (C) its corresponding calibration curve and linear relation.
(A) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CoOOH and (B) GSH detection based on CoOOH and metal indicator.
Detection of GSH in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets integrating with metal indicator enable sensitive detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pesticide and Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen, 448000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen, 448000, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) plays a significant role in various biological functions. The fluctuation of GSH level is closely linked to some serious diseases. Currently, the colorimetric detection of GSH is mainly built on the inhibitory effect on the catalytic activity of the developed nanomaterials. However, tedious synthesis of the nanomaterials is always required to control their morphologies for better catalytic activity. Metal indicators are some specific substances capable of chelating metal ions, during which significant color variation can be triggered, making them promising for colorimetric analysis. More importantly, the recognition of metal ions has nothing to do with the structure of the materials. Herein, CoOOH ultrathin nanosheets were prepared via a facile method, which can be easily reduced to Co2+ ions by GSH. After being chelated by metal indicator, a rapid and sensitive sensing platform for GSH detection is established with the distinct color change. As a result, the UV–vis absorbances of Co2+–xylenol orange disodium salt complexes (a typical metal indicator) at 580 nm exhibit good linearity against GSH concentrations from 33 μM to 1.3 mM with the detection limit of 13.7 μM. The developed sensor is further used in the detection of GSH contained in the human serum and an ideal recovery rate is obtained, demonstrating its excellent applicability for the detection of GSH in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is an immanent nonprotein thiol which plays important roles in various biological functions [1–3], including intracellular signal transduction, genetic regulation, and xenobiotic metabolism [4,5]. The changes of GSH level are found closely associated with some serious diseases, such as leukopenia, parapsoriasis guttata, hepatic injury, Parkinson's, and so like [6,7]. Especially, the concentration of GSH in human serum has demonstrated a correlation with cancer pathogenesis [8,9]. Therefore, great efforts have been made to develop effective strategies for sensitive and selective testing of GSH in vitro or in serum samples, including fluorescence [10–12], colorimetry [13–15], electrochemistry [16,17], photoelectrochemistry [18], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [19,20], and so on. Among them, colorimetric detection of GSH has been extensively explored due to its convenience to acquire the signals which can be even observed by naked eyes. For example, a rapid and sensitive colorimetric method based on a peroxidase mimic of tungsten disulfide was designed for detecting GSH in serum samples with an extremely low limit of detection (61 pM) due to the inhibiting ability of GSH to the catalytic activity of WS2 [21]. In addition, another colorimetric GSH detection method was presented using ultrafine and monodispersed iridium nanoparticles doped nitrogen-functional carbon materials [22]. In the presence of GSH, the TMB oxidation reaction was prevented, triggering the color fading of the solution. Although this inhibiting type strategy exhibited a detection limit of 0.5 μM, a slight color change of the solution was not in favor of the sensitive detection of GSH at an ultralow concentration. Despite the great efforts in the detection of GSH, most of the detection strategies are based on the inhibitory effect of GSH on the catalytic activity of the as-prepared nanomaterials. Moreover, the tedious synthesis of the nanomaterials is always required to control their size, morphology, composition and structure for better catalytic activity. Thus, more convenient sensing platforms for GSH detection are desperately needed.

Metal indicators, such as xylenol orange sodium salt (XO), 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN), and Eriochrome Black T (EBT), are capable of chelating metal ions with the color change, which can realize the sensitive detection of metal ions. For instance, XO and PAN change from yellow to purple and red after chelating metal ions, respectively, while the color of EBT changes from blue to purple. Notably, the color of metal indicator changes instantly when chelating with metal ions and the color can keep stable for a long time, which is beneficial to the following detection and observation. Therefore, developing sensors with the colorimetric method based on the metal indicator is promising to fulfill faster detection compared with the commonly used color developing agents, such as 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). As a typical 2D nanomaterial, CoOOH can be facilely synthesized and has been widely used in sensing [23], electrocatalysis [24–29], environmental remediation [30], and so on. Up to now, some specific biomarkers, such as methyltransferase [23], ascorbic acid [31], alkaline phosphatase [32], vitamin C [33], have been sensitively detected based on CoOOH nanosheets with fluorescent and photoelectrochemical methods [34]. Regardless of the oxidative nature of CoOOH, the reported sensors for GSH with CoOOH ultrathin nanosheets are scarce, let alone a colorimetric method.

With the above consideration in mind, a colorimetric strategy is innovatively proposed to quantitatively detect GSH by virtue of the merits of metal indicator and CoOOH nanosheet (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, CoOOH nanosheets were firstly synthesized by a hydrothermal method (Scheme 1A). Then, the as-prepared CoOOH was incubated with GSH for about 20 min, during which CoOOH can be reduced into Co2+ and the free Co2+ ions were produced and subsequently chelated by XO with remarkable color changes. After that, the UV–vis measurement of Co2+@XO was conducted to evaluate the concentrations of GSH. Compared with the reported tedious strategies for sensing GSH [35,36], this novel strategy based on the metal indicator and CoOOH nanosheet is simple, flexible and time-saving, which can be expanded into the detection of other biomolecules.

Glutathione (Reduced) (GSH), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), glucose, 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4•2H2O), Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4•12H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2•6H2O), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) were bought from Singpharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. 1-(2-pyridinylazo)-2-naohthalenol (PAN), xylenol orange disodium salt (XO), and eriochrome black T (EBT) were purchased from Jiangsu luye biotechnology Co. Ltd. The serum samples were collected and purified by the cooperative hospital and stored at −20 °C. The serum samples were used without further purification.

All UV–vis spectrals were obtained by a multimode reader (Tecan Spark, Switzerland). The transmission electron microscope (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and high‐angle annular dark‐field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF‐STEM) images were obtained on a Titan G260-300. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was measured by a D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were acquired by a TENSOR27 (Bruker, United States). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were operated on ESCALAB 250X (Thermo Fisher, United States).

CoOOH was synthesized by a facile and time-efficient approach. Firstly, 10 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (1 M) and 10 mL sodium hypochlorite solution (30 wt%) were mixed in a beaker. Then, 10 mL of CoCl2•6H2O solution (10 mM) was added into the above solution and sonicated for 1 min at room temperature. The dark brown CoOOH was obtained by centrifugation at 10 000 rpm for 4 min and dispersed in 20 mL water. Subsequently, CoOOH was further sonicated by Cell fragmentation apparatus for another 1 h while keeping the temperature below 20 °C. Then, it was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO1000, Spectrum). The resultant product solution was dispersed in 25 mL distilled water and was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The colorimetric test of Co2+ was confirmed by the color change of XO before and after chelating Co2+. The characteristic absorption peak was at 580 nm. The concentrations of XO was 2.5 mM. Specifically, 100 μL of Co2+ and 100 μL of Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 (PBS) (0.2 M, pH 6.0) were added in the mixture of 10 μL of XO and 100 μL of PBS. The time-dependent absorbances were calculated by the following formula.
Relative Activity = Abst /Absmax.

Colorimetric tests of TMB was determined by the color variation of TMB when oxidized by H2O2 under the catalysis of HRP. The characteristic absorption peak of TMB was at 652 nm. The concentrations of TMB and H2O2 were 1 mM. The concentration of HRP was 10 μg/mL. As for the colorimetric detection of TMB, 100 μL of TMB solution and 100 μL of H2O2 solution were mixed with 10 μL of HRP and 100 μL of HAc-NaAc buffered solution (0.1 M, pH 3.0). Similarly, the relative activity was calculated by the same formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing of GSH in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL of GSH with various concentrations and 100 μL of CoOOH (1 mM) were added into the 96-microwell plates which containing 100 μL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 6.0). After the mixtures were incubated for about 10 min at 37 °C, the absorbance values between 300 nm and 1000 nm were recorded by a multimode reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practical usability of this strategy, 100 μL of human serum mixed with 100 μL of GSH under different concentrations were prepared as real samples. During the test, 100 μL of CoOOH (1 mM) was dropped in the mixture and incubated for 10 min at 37 °C, and XO (2.5 mM) was added in the mixture. The absorbance values were recorded and the recovery of GSH in human serum was figured out.

CoOOH were synthesized by a facile approach. Firstly, a certain amount of NaOH and NaClO were mixed to form a solution with a strong oxidizing ability. Then, CoCl2•6H2O was added to the above mixture, and bulk CoOOH was generated instantaneously. CoOOH nanosheet was obtained after exfoliation with the ultrasonic treatment. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the morphology of ultrathin nanosheets was characterized by TEM. Digital photograph in Fig. 1A (inset) shows Tyndall effect of CoOOH, demonstrating the colloidal properties and excellent dispersion. As expected, the colloidal CoOOH can keep stable for months. The HRTEM image of the as-prepared CoOOH was shown in Fig. 1B, revealing the disordered structure. The SAED pattern in Fig. 1C displays ring-like features that indicating the poor crystallinity of CoOOH, which is in accordance with the HRTEM result. HAADF-STEM mapping images (Fig. 1D, E and F) manifest the uniform distribution of Co and O elements within CoOOH framework. Based on these results, the CoOOH with unordered lamellar structure is successfully synthesized.

More detailed information on the structure and element valences were shown in Fig. 2 . No obvious peak presents in the XRD pattern in Fig. 2A, indicating the disordered structure of the as-prepared CoOOH [37], which is consistent with the SAED result mentioned above. The FT-IR spectrum was obtained to explore the molecular structure and chemical composition of CoOOH. In Fig. 2B, the peaks at 3423, 1629–1332 and 598 cm−1 can be observed, which corresponding to H-bonded hydroxyl group (OH) on the surface of CoOOH, CoO double bond in the structure of CoOOH and Co-O2- stretching vibration [38], respectively. The UV–vis absorbance peak of CoOOH located at about 358 nm (Fig. 2B inset). The chemical element and oxidation state were further investigated by XPS (Fig. S1), confirming the presence of Co and O. The high-resolution spectrum of Co 2p (Fig. 2C) shows the peaks of Co 2p1/2 (796.83 eV) and Co 2p3/2 (780.92) with two satellite peaks at 804.36 and 789.59 eV, which are categorized as the CoIII of CoOOH [39]. Two peaks for O 1s at 530.79 and 529.49 eV are observed for CoOOH in Fig. 2D, which corresponds to the CoOH bond and CoO bond of CoOOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color changes of metal indicator in the presence of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the color change, the UV–vis absorbance of the metal indicators of PAN, EBT and XO with Co2+ were recorded, where the concentrations of PAN, EBT and XO were 4 mM and the concentration of Co2+ was 1 mM. The largest absorbance variations of PAN, EBT and XO occurred at the wavelength of 614 nm, 624 nm and 580 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 A, Fig. S2A). Among the three metal indicators mentioned above, XO exhibits the best contrast in color after being complexed with Co2+, which is chosen for further investigation. When the concentration of Co2+ changed from 0.1 μM to 5 mM, the color of Co2+@XO gradually changed from yellow to purple (Fig. 3B). The maximum absorption wavelength at 580 nm is due to the metal-to-ligand charge-transfer (MLCT) [40]. The color of the complex was closely related to the concentration of Co2+ (Fig. S2B). Compared with some inhibitory methods that rely on the color change of TMB, the reaction of the metal indicator is faster, and the corresponding absorption value reaches a peak rapidly (Fig. 3C). Moreover, the color of Co2+@XO can last for a long time for the good stability of the complex [41].

By taking advantage of the outstanding oxidation property of CoOOH, the colorimetric biosensing of GSH based on CoOOH was further performed. The concentration of CoOOH and XO were 1 mM and 2.5 mM, respectively. After incubated with GSH, Co2+ was released from the framework of CoOOH. As presented in Fig. 2B inset and Fig. 4 A, the UV–vis absorbance peak of CoOOH located at 358 nm, after been reduced by GSH, the color of CoOOH faded and the absorbance values at the wavelength of 358 nm decreased. The signal was enlarged by the addition of XO at 580 nm as shown in Fig. 4A.

To construct a sensitive sensor for GSH analysis, the experimental condition was optimized subsequently. As displayed in Fig. S3A, the absorbance of Co2+@XO enhanced with the increase of the concentration of XO. When the concentration of XO reached 3 mM, the value of the absorbance was too large to be read by the multimode reader. To facilitate further measurement, the concentration of XO was selected to be 2.5 mM. In addition, the incubating temperature (Fig. S3B), incubating time (Fig. S3C) and CoOOH concentration (Fig. S3D) were also optimized. As a result, an incubating temperature of 37 °C, an incubating time of 10 min and a concentration of CoOOH at 1 mM was acquired to carry out the tests. Furthermore, the pH value of the solution can also influence the testing results. As shown in Fig. S4A, the color of XO changed obviously after complexed with Co2+ at the pH of 6 and 7. However, the color of XO itself was already purple when the pH was 7, which was not an optimal color. To stabilize the CoOOH, the solution with a higher pH value of 6 was used (Fig. S4B). Under optimal conditions, the response to GSH was investigated. By increasing the GSH concentration, the absorbance values generated by Co2+@XO complex increased accordingly (Fig. 4B). Finally, a relationship between GSH concentration and the absorbance values was achieved between 33 μM and 1.7 mM. Due to a high concentration of GSH in human serum (mM levels), this range is favorable. Relevant calibration curve based on the absorbance values of Co2+@XO was presented in Fig. 4C, which can be described with two linear parts from 33 μM to 1.3 mM with the inflection point occurring at 333 μM. The regression equations shown in Fig. 4C were used as calibration for further calculations in practical detection. Noteworthily, an ultralow limit of detection (LOD) of 13.7 μM (correlation coefficient is 0.99, S/N = 3) is realized for sensing GSH. Compared with other reported GSH sensing methods in Tab. S1, the as-prepared sensor is among the best-known sensitive sensors. Remarkably, the colorimetric method based on CoOOH and metal indicator is simple and time-saving, which makes it an anticipated method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the practical applications, the determination of GSH contained in serum samples was performed. In detail, 100 μL of human serum samples were incubated with 100 μL of CoOOH (1 mM) and 100 μL of GSH standard solution with different concentrations for 10 min, then XO (2.5 mM) was mixed with the mixture. After that, the UV–vis absorbance at 580 nm was read by a multimode reader (Table 1 ). Delightfully, the recoveries for these samples are in the range of 96.06 %–114 %, indicating the feasibility of the strategy combining CoOOH with the metal indicator.

In addition, the stability and reproducibility of the CoOOH for GSH detection were evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the absorbance of Co2+@XO still kept 93 % of the original values after 28 days, indicating the excellent stability of CoOOH. Furthermore, to evaluate the reproducibility of CoOOH, the nanosheets were prepared by the same method five times and the absorbance values of Co2+@XO were almost the same, demonstrating the remarkable reproducibility (Fig. 5B). Moreover, several commonly used interfering substrates were employed to study the selectivity of CoOOH for GSH detection. Compared with GSH (3 mM), d-Glucose, K+ and Mg2+, whose concentrations were 30 mM, did not cause distinct color changes (Fig. S5).

In this work, a novel strategy of combining metal indicators with CoOOH to detect the GSH level was developed. CoOOH with disordered structure was successfully prepared via a facile method and its amorphous nature was further proved by TEM, HRTEM, and XRD pattern, which can be easily reduced by GSH. Based on the sensitive recognition of metal ions with metal indicator, an excellent linear relationship can be achieved for the detection of metal ions. Benefiting from the admirable oxidation property of CoOOH, Co2+ ions can be produced by GSH in human serum from cancer patients. As expected, the as-prepared CoOOH was used for the colorimetric biosensing of GSH in vitro with a good linear relationship between the UV–vis absorbance of Co2+@XO and the concentration of GSH. Furthermore, the recovery of GSH in human serum samples is demonstrated, indicating this strategy is promising for real sample detection.

Jinli Li: Investigation, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Writing - review & editing. Lei Jiao: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Data curation. Weiqing Xu: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Hongye Yan: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Guojuan Chen: Writing - review & editing. Yu Wu: Writing - review & editing. Liuyong Hu: Supervision, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Wenling Gu: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the self-determined research funds of CCNU from the colleges' basic research and operation of MOE (CCNU18JCXK07), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (CCNU20QN007), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2020CFB276).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2020.129247.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: XPS pattern of CoOOH; UV–vis absorption spectra of PAN, Co2++PAN, EBT, Co2++EBT, XO, Co2++XO and the photographs; Calibration curve of UV–vis absorption spectra of XO complexed with Co2+ with different concentrations; Optimization of XO concentration, incubate temperature, incubate time and CoOOH concentration; Effect of pH on the UV–vis absorbance of XO, Co2+@XO, CoOOH and CoOOH+XO; Selectivity; Comparison of different methods for GSH determination.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for glutathione detection based on poly-L-lysine as co-reactant and graphene-based poly(luminol/aniline) as nanoprobes
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Wang, L.M. Chen, P.J. Wang, M.S. Li, D.F. Liu, A novel ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for glutathione detection based on poly-L-lysine as co-reactant and graphene-based poly(luminol/aniline) as nanoprobes, Biosens. Bioelectron. 133 (2019) 154-159.

Anal. Chem.

Rev. Anal. Chem.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Anal. Chem.

Chem. Commun.

Chem. Sci.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinli Li received her BS Degree (2009) from Qilu University of Technology and continued her postgraduate study under the supervision of Associate Professor Quanxiao Liu at Beijing Institute of Graphic Communication. She is currently pursuing her Ph. D study under the supervision of Prof. Chengzhou Zhu at Central China Normal University. Her research is primarily on the analytical chemistry and sensors.

Liuyong Hu received his B.S. degree from Hefei University of Technology in 2012. He then joined the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences as a Ph.D. candidate, under the supervision of Professor Zhi Yuan Wang. After that, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow under the supervision of Professor He Yan at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology. Currently, he is the associate professor at Wuhan Institute of Technology. His current research interest focuses on nanomaterials and conjugated polymers-based semiconductors for electrochemical and analytical applications.

Wenling Gu received her B.S. degree from Northwest Normal University in 2012. She then joined the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences as a Ph.D. candidate, under the supervision of Professor Erkang Wang. After that, she worked as a postdoctoral fellow under the supervision of Professor Tianshou Zhao at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology. Currently, she is the associate professor at Central China Normal University. Her current research interest focuses on carbon and metal nanomaterials for electrochemical and analytical applications.
